name: Beefsteak Smothered in Onions (Filet Braise Aux Ognons)
recipeInstructions:
1. Beat the round steak well with a rolling pin or steak hammer; cut off the outer skin and press the meat back into shape.
2. Place the lard in a deep frying pan and let it melt.
3. Lay in the sliced onions, then place the beefsteak on top of them. Season the steak with salt and pepper, and dredge it with flour.
4. Cover closely and let simmer over hot fire for a few minutes.
5. Turn the steak to the other side. After three minutes, add the vinegar, chopped parsley, thyme, bay leaf, and garlic.
6. Continue cooking, turning occasionally so that the flour browns well, and keep the pot covered.
7. When browned, pour in one cup of water (or a pint) to cover the meat. Bring this to a brisk boil.
8. Reduce heat to allow it to simmer gently for about two hours.
This introduction precedes the recipe:
Smothered Steak (Filet Braise)
Braising or smothering meat is a mode of cooking little understood by Americans, but which has been brought by the Creoles to a high state of perfection. By this process the meat is just covered, with no more than that, either with water or a strong broth made from animal stock or vegetable juices. The pot is covered tightly and placed on a slow fire, allowing it to simmer gently for two or three hours, just short of boiling. This slow cooking method makes tough meats juicy, tender, and very palatable, while the sealed pot retains all the flavor.
The secret in smothering meat lies in letting it cook very slowly while still simmering so that heat can penetrate thoroughly and render the meat's coarse fibers tender and juicy. When tender, serve the beefsteak on a platter with onions and gravy for a delicious, delicately flavored dish.
